title: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis
authors: Avindra Nath, Walter J. Koroshetz, Michael R. Wilson

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Chronic Meningitis
items.content: Chronic meningitis is defined by the presence of a characteristic neurologic syndrome lasting more than 4 weeks, accompanied by persistent inflammation in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). The diagnostic threshold for CSF white cell count indicative of chronic meningitis is greater than 5/µL.
keyPoints: Chronic meningitis diagnosis requires >4 weeks of neurologic syndrome
Persistent inflammatory response in CSF with WBC count >5/µL
Identification of etiology is crucial for appropriate treatment
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis

items.title: Etiologies of Chronic Meningitis
items.content: The causes of chronic meningitis are diverse, but five categories account for most cases: meningeal infections, malignancy, autoimmune inflammatory disorders, chemical meningitis, and parameningeal infections. Additionally, monogenic autoinflammatory disorders are increasingly recognized as a cause in patients with recurrent meningitis.
keyPoints: Five main categories of disease causing chronic meningitis
Categories: meningeal infections, malignancy, autoimmune inflammatory disorders, chemical meningitis, parameningeal infections
Monogenic autoinflammatory disorders are emerging causes for recurrent cases
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis

items.title: Diagnosis and Pathophysiology of Chronic Meningitis
items.content: Chronic meningitis is diagnosed when clinical symptoms prompt CSF examination for inflammation. The condition involves the spread of inflammatory processes via CSF pathways, affecting various neural structures.
keyPoints: CSF produced by choroid plexus and resorbed by arachnoid villi
Inflammation can cause headache, hydrocephalus, cranial neuropathies, radiculopathies, cognitive changes
Meningeal signs are uncommon in chronic meningitis
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CLINICAL PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Neurologic Manifestations of Intracranial Meningitis
items.content: Intracranial meningitis results from stimulation of nociceptive nerve fibers in the meninges, leading to symptoms such as headache and signs of raised intracranial pressure. It can also cause cognitive changes due to vascular damage.
keyPoints: Headache, neck/back pain, hydrocephalus, raised ICP
Symptoms: vomiting, apathy, gait instability, papilledema, visual loss, impaired upgaze, CN VI palsy
Cognitive/behavioral changes from vascular damage
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CLINICAL PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Spinal Meningitis and Its Neurologic Impact
items.content: Spinal meningitis affects motor and sensory nerve roots, leading to radiculopathies. It can cause arachnoiditis and myelopathy due to meningeal inflammation.
keyPoints: Multiple radiculopathies with pain, sensory loss, weakness
Arachnoiditis causes clumping of lower nerve roots
Involvement of spinal cord results in myelopathy
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CLINICAL PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Skin Changes in Chronic Meningitis: Diagnostic Clues and Red Flags
items.content: Certain skin manifestations can be indicative of underlying causes of chronic meningitis. Recognizing these patterns is crucial for guiding further diagnostic evaluation.
keyPoints: Behçet's syndrome, systemic lupus erythematosus, Lyme disease, sporotrichosis, and cryptococcosis are associated with skin changes in chronic meningitis.
NOMID (neonatal-onset multisystem inflammatory disease) is another condition linked to skin manifestations.
Trypanosomiasis, blastomycosis, sarcoidosis, vasculitis, CAPS (urticaria), and IV drug use are additional causes of skin changes in chronic meningitis.
Syphilis can present with diffuse rash including palms and soles.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs

items.title: Bone/Marrow Involvement in Chronic Meningitis: Key Considerations
items.content: In chronic meningitis, involvement of bone or bone marrow can suggest specific etiologies that require targeted diagnostic approaches.
keyPoints: ANCA associated vasculitis is linked to bone/bone marrow changes in the context of chronic meningitis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs

items.title: Identifying Etiologies of Chronic Meningitis with Recurrent Episodes
items.content: Chronic meningitis can present as recurrent discrete episodes, where symptoms and CSF parameters resolve completely between episodes. This pattern suggests specific etiologies such as Mollaret's meningitis (often due to HSV-2), chemical meningitis from tumors or cysts, primary autoimmune conditions, drug hypersensitivity, or inherited autoinflammatory syndromes.
keyPoints: Recurrent discrete episodes of illness with complete resolution between episodes

Likely etiologies include Mollaret's meningitis (HSV-2), chemical meningitis from tumors/cysts, autoimmune conditions like Behçet's syndrome and SLE, drug hypersensitivity, inherited autoinflammatory syndromes
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Genitals

items.title: Epidemiologic History in Diagnosing Chronic Meningitis
items.content: A detailed epidemiologic history is crucial for diagnosing chronic meningitis. It helps guide laboratory studies by considering past exposures and travel to endemic areas, which can indicate specific infectious or inflammatory causes.
keyPoints: Importance of epidemiologic history in diagnosis

Considerations include tuberculosis exposure, fungal infection-endemic areas (e.g., San Joaquin Valley for coccidioidomycosis), Brucella from unpasteurized dairy, Lyme disease from wooded areas, and more
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Genitals

items.title: Clinical Signs Suggesting Specific Causes of Chronic Meningitis
items.content: Certain clinical signs can suggest specific causes of chronic meningitis. For example, focal cerebral signs may indicate a brain abscess or infarct, while aphthous oral lesions and genital ulcers suggest Behçet's syndrome.
keyPoints: Focal cerebral signs suggest brain abscess or infarct
Aphthous oral lesions, genital ulcers, hypopyon suggest Behçet's syndrome
Hepatosplenomegaly suggests lymphoma, sarcoid, tuberculosis, or brucellosis
Arthralgias and gastrointestinal symptoms may indicate Lyme disease or Whipple's disease
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Genitals

items.title: When to Perform Brain Imaging Before Lumbar Puncture in Chronic Meningitis
items.content: Brain imaging is crucial before lumbar puncture if there's a risk of raised intracranial pressure (ICP) due to conditions like mass lesions, brain swelling, or obstructive hydrocephalus. This precaution prevents the potential risk of brain herniation during lumbar puncture.
keyPoints: Perform imaging before LP if raised ICP is suspected due to mass lesion, brain swelling, or obstructive hydrocephalus.
Lumbar puncture carries a risk of brain herniation in cases of elevated ICP from these conditions.
Obstructive hydrocephalus usually requires direct ventricular drainage.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · IMAGING

items.title: Role of Contrast-Enhanced MRI or CT in Chronic Meningitis Diagnosis
items.content: Contrast-enhanced MRI or CT is used to identify meningeal enhancement, parameningeal infections, spinal cord encasement, and nodular deposits on the meninges. These imaging techniques guide biopsy and help visualize cysts causing chemical meningitis.
keyPoints: Identify meningeal enhancement, parameningeal infections (e.g., brain abscess), and encasement of the spinal cord.
Detect nodular deposits on meninges or nerve roots due to malignancy, sarcoidosis, or schistosomiasis.
Guide biopsy of affected meninges and visualize cysts causing chemical meningitis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · IMAGING

items.title: When to Consider Empiric Treatment for Neurosyphilis Despite Negative CSF VDRL
items.content: In cases where a patient is at high risk and has positive treponemal antibodies in the serum along with unexplained CSF pleocytosis, empiric treatment for neurosyphilis may be appropriate even if the CSF VDRL test is negative. This decision is based on the specificity of the CSF VDRL test for syphilis.
keyPoints: CSF VDRL is highly specific but not particularly sensitive for syphilis.
Empiric treatment for neurosyphilis may be appropriate in high-risk patients with negative CSF VDRL, positive serum treponemal antibodies, and unexplained CSF pleocytosis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID ANALYSIS

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Chronic Meningitis Based on Cell Predominance in CSF
items.content: In chronic meningitis, the type of cells predominating in the CSF guides differential diagnosis. Mononuclear cell predominance suggests various infectious and noninfectious causes, while neutrophil predominance after 3 weeks points to specific pathogens or conditions.
keyPoints: Mononuclear cells predominate in most categories of chronic meningitis.
Neutrophils predominating after 3 weeks suggest Nocardia asteroides, Actinomyces israelii, Brucella, M. tuberculosis (5–10% early cases), fungi, or noninfectious causes like SLE.
Eosinophils in CSF indicate parasitic diseases, fungal infections, neoplastic disease, or other inflammatory processes.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID ANALYSIS

items.title: When to Pursue Systemic Infection in Chronic Meningitis
items.content: In chronic meningitis, systemic investigations are crucial when CSF findings suggest an underlying condition. This includes tests like chest radiographs and urine cultures, which can identify pulmonary foci of infection, particularly fungal or tuberculous diseases.
keyPoints: Pulmonary foci of infection may be present in fungal or tuberculous disease.
CT or MRI of the chest and sputum examination are helpful.
Bronchoscopy or transthoracic needle biopsy can pursue abnormalities.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.title: Utility of Tuberculin Skin Test in Chronic Meningitis
items.content: The tuberculin skin test is often used to screen for tuberculosis in chronic meningitis, though it has limited specificity and sensitivity. Gamma interferon release assays are preferred where available for diagnosing latent tuberculosis.
keyPoints: Tuberculin skin test has limited specificity and sensitivity for active disease.
Gamma interferon release assays may be used to diagnose latent tuberculosis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.question: Which of the following criteria must be met for a diagnosis of chronic meningitis?
options: A characteristic neurologic syndrome exists for more than 4 weeks with a persistent inflammatory response in the CSF (white cell count >5/µL)
A characteristic neurologic syndrome exists for less than 2 weeks with an increased white blood cell count
Presence of fever and headache without any specific findings in the cerebrospinal fluid
Identification of a bacterial pathogen in the cerebrospinal fluid
items.explanation: Chronic meningitis is diagnosed when a characteristic neurologic syndrome exists for >4 weeks with a persistent inflammatory response in the CSF (white cell count >5/µL).
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis

items.question: Which category of disease is NOT typically associated with chronic meningitis?
items.explanation: Chronic meningitis is usually caused by meningeal infections, malignancy, autoimmune inflammatory disorders, chemical meningitis, and parameningeal infections, not respiratory tract infections.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis

items.question: What recent recognition has been made regarding patients with recurrent meningitis?
options: They may have monogenic autoinflammatory disorders
They are likely to develop chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
They often suffer from gastrointestinal infections
They usually have a history of viral encephalitis
items.explanation: There is increasing recognition that some patients with recurrent meningitis may have monogenic autoinflammatory disorders.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis

items.question: Which of the following is a common feature of intracranial meningitis?
options: Headache, neck pain, or back pain due to stimulation of nociceptive nerve fibers in the meninges.
Cognitive changes result primarily from direct bacterial invasion of brain tissue.
Hydrocephalus is caused by obstruction at the arachnoid villi.
items.explanation: Intracranial meningitis stimulates nociceptive nerve fibers in the meninges, causing headache, neck pain, or back pain.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CLINICAL PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a likely cause of cognitive and behavioral changes during chronic meningitis?
options: Vascular damage due to inflammation around blood vessels in the subarachnoid space causing infarction.
Obstruction of CSF pathways at the cerebral aqueduct.
Inflammatory deposits in the frontal and temporal lobes.
items.explanation: Cognitive and behavioral changes can result from vascular damage due to inflammation around blood vessels, causing infarction.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CLINICAL PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: In spinal meningitis, what is a common presentation?
options: Cognitive and behavioral changes due to inflammation around the brainstem.
Multiple radiculopathies with combinations of radicular pain, sensory loss, motor weakness, and urinary or fecal incontinence.
Headache and vomiting due to raised intracranial pressure.
items.explanation: Spinal meningitis often presents as multiple radiculopathies with combinations of symptoms such as radicular pain, sensory loss, motor weakness, and urinary or fecal incontinence.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CLINICAL PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: What is a significant concern for immunosuppressed patients regarding chronic meningitis?
options: Chronic meningitis may be caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis or Cryptococcus neoformans and can present without headache, fever, or meningeal signs.
Cognitive changes are the primary concern.
Hydrocephalus is a common initial presentation.
items.explanation: In immunosuppressed patients, chronic meningitis may be caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis or Cryptococcus neoformans and can present without typical symptoms like headache, fever, or meningeal signs.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CLINICAL PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs
items.question: Which condition associated with chronic meningitis is known to cause skin changes including lesions on the palms and soles?
items.explanation: Syphilis can cause diffuse skin rashes including on the palms and soles.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs

items.subtopic: Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs
items.question: Which condition listed under 'Bone/Bone marrow' is associated with chronic meningitis?
items.explanation: ANCA associated vasculitis is listed under 'Bone/Bone marrow' in relation to chronic meningitis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs

items.subtopic: Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs
items.question: Which of the following conditions can cause sinus involvement leading to chronic meningitis?
items.explanation: All listed conditions can cause sinus involvement leading to chronic meningitis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs

items.question: Which of the following is most often associated with Mollaret's meningitis?
items.explanation: Mollaret's meningitis is most often due to herpes simplex virus (HSV) type 2.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Genitals

items.question: Which condition can cause recurrent meningitis and involves inherited autoinflammatory syndromes?
options: Tumor necrosis factor receptor-associated periodic syndrome (TRAPS)
items.explanation: Inherited autoinflammatory syndromes like TRAPS can cause recurrent meningitis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Genitals

items.question: Which of the following is a likely etiology for recurrent episodes of chemical meningitis?
items.explanation: Chemical meningitis can be due to episodic leakage from an epidermoid tumor.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Genitals

items.question: Which of the following conditions is associated with genital ulcers, aphthous oral lesions, and hypopyon?
items.explanation: Aphthous oral lesions, genital ulcers, and hypopyon suggest Behçet's syndrome.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Genitals

items.question: Which imaging study should be performed before lumbar puncture if there is a possibility of raised intracranial pressure (ICP)?
items.explanation: A brain imaging study should be performed before lumbar puncture if there is a possibility of raised ICP to avoid the risk of brain herniation.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · IMAGING

items.question: In patients with open CSF flow pathways and elevated ICP, what procedure may be necessary to prevent abrupt deterioration?
items.explanation: In patients with open CSF flow pathways, repetitive or continuous lumbar drainage may be necessary to prevent abrupt deterioration from raised ICP.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · IMAGING

items.question: What can contrast-enhanced MRI or CT studies identify in the context of chronic meningitis?
options: Direct visualization of CSF flow pathways
items.explanation: Contrast-enhanced MRI or CT studies can identify meningeal enhancement, parameningeal infections, and other structural abnormalities in chronic meningitis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · IMAGING

items.question: Which test is highly specific but not particularly sensitive for syphilis when performed on CSF?
items.explanation: The CSF VDRL test is highly specific but not particularly sensitive for syphilis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID ANALYSIS

items.question: What type of cells predominantly appear in the CSF in most categories of chronic meningitis?
items.explanation: In most categories of chronic meningitis, mononuclear cells predominate in the CSF.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID ANALYSIS

items.question: Which method can be used to detect a broad range of bacterial causes of meningitis and is particularly useful when culture yield is low?
items.explanation: 16s ribosomal RNA (rRNA) PCR can be used to detect a broad range of bacterial causes of meningitis and is particularly useful in partially treated meningitis when the yield of culture is low.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID ANALYSIS

items.question: Which test is often used to diagnose latent tuberculosis but has limited specificity and sensitivity for active disease?
items.explanation: The tuberculin skin test is often used but has limited specificity and sensitivity for active tuberculosis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.question: Which imaging study may be helpful in identifying pulmonary foci of infection, particularly with fungal or tuberculous disease?
options: CT or MRI of the chest
items.explanation: A CT or MRI of the chest may be helpful in identifying pulmonary foci of infection.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.question: In cases where a systemic site of infection is suspected, which procedure might be used to pursue abnormalities?
items.explanation: Bronchoscopy or transthoracic needle biopsy may be used to pursue abnormalities in suspected systemic infections.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.statement: Chemical meningitis can be a cause of chronic meningitis.
items.explanation: Chemical meningitis is one of the five categories that account for most cases of chronic meningitis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis

items.statement: A white cell count less than 5/µL in CSF rules out chronic meningitis.
items.explanation: Chronic meningitis is associated with a persistent inflammatory response in the CSF, indicated by a white cell count >5/µL.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis

items.statement: Meningeal signs are common in chronic meningitis.
items.explanation: Meningeal signs are uncommon in chronic meningitis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CLINICAL PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: The cerebral lymphatic system's role in chronic meningitis has been extensively studied.
items.explanation: The role of the recently identified cerebral lymphatic system in chronic meningitis has not been studied.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CLINICAL PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs
items.statement: Herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2 can cause oral manifestations in chronic meningitis.
items.explanation: Herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2 are listed under 'Mouth' as causing oral manifestations.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs

items.subtopic: Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs
items.statement: IgG4-related disease is associated with heart/lung involvement in chronic meningitis.
items.explanation: IgG4-related diseases are listed under 'Heart/Lungs' as being involved.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs

items.statement: Herpetic lesions in the genital area or on the thighs are indicative of HSV-1 infection.
items.explanation: Herpetic lesions in these areas suggest HSV-2 infection.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Genitals

items.statement: Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome can be associated with uveitis.
items.explanation: Ophthalmologic examination may reveal uveitis in Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Genitals

items.statement: In patients with obstructive hydrocephalus, lumbar puncture is usually safe.
items.explanation: Lumbar puncture in patients with obstructive hydrocephalus carries the risk of brain herniation due to elevated ICP.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · IMAGING

items.statement: Angiographic studies can identify cerebral arteritis in patients with chronic meningitis and stroke.
items.explanation: Angiographic studies are useful for identifying evidence of cerebral arteritis in such patients.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · IMAGING

items.statement: In patients with suspected fungal infections, CSF assays for beta-glucans are never useful.
items.explanation: CSF assays for beta-glucans may be a useful adjunct in establishing the diagnosis of fungal infections when other tests are negative.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID ANALYSIS

items.statement: In chronic meningitis, neutrophils predominate after 3 weeks of illness.
items.explanation: When neutrophils predominate after 3 weeks of illness in chronic meningitis, it suggests certain etiologic considerations like Nocardia asteroides or fungal infections.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID ANALYSIS

items.statement: A tuberculin skin test is highly specific and sensitive for diagnosing active tuberculosis.
items.explanation: The tuberculin skin test has limited specificity and sensitivity for the diagnosis of active tuberculosis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.statement: Gamma interferon release assays can be used to diagnose latent tuberculosis.
items.explanation: Gamma interferon release assays are available for diagnosing latent tuberculosis.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · LABORATORY INVESTIGATION

items.reason: Obstruction of CSF pathways at the cerebral aqueduct or arachnoid villi may produce hydrocephalus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CLINICAL PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.assertion: In spinal meningitis, involvement of lower nerve roots is rare.
items.reason: Inflammatory cells gravitate to the bottom of the intrathecal space causing preferential involvement of lower nerve roots.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; involvement of lower nerve roots in spinal meningitis is common due to inflammatory cells gravitating to the bottom of the intrathecal space.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CLINICAL PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs
items.assertion: Systemic lupus erythematosus can cause both skin and oral manifestations in chronic meningitis.
items.reason: Systemic lupus erythematosus is listed under 'Skin changes' and 'Mouth'.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Chronic Meningitis | Skin changes | Bone/Bone marrow | Sinus | Mouth | Heart/Lungs

items.reason: Dural enhancement after lumbar puncture, neurosurgical procedures, concussion, or spontaneous CSF leakage can cause meningeal enhancement without being concerning.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because dural enhancement in certain conditions is not always concerning.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Genitals

items.assertion: Behçet's syndrome may present with genital ulcers and uveitis.
items.reason: Behçet's syndrome can cause aphthous oral lesions, genital ulcers, hypopyon, and iridocyclitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · Genitals

items.assertion: Repeated samples of large volumes of lumbar CSF may be required to diagnose certain infectious and malignant causes of chronic meningitis.
items.reason: Lymphomatous or carcinomatous meningitis can be diagnosed by examining sections cut from a cell block formed by spinning down the sediment from a large volume of CSF.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Chronic and Recurrent Meningitis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID ANALYSIS
